A Monster with this attribute has a Magical Dispel Rating (n+), that is the number required on a roll of 1D6 for the ability to work. The dice roll is made as soon as the targeted spell is cast. If the roll is equal to or greater than the Monster's Magic Dispel Rating, the spell has no effect at all and the Power used to cast it is wasted.

Some Monsters can soak up or Drain Magic from the air around them, rendering any spellcasting impossible while they remain alive. A Monster with this attribute has a Magic Drain Rating (n+), which is the number required on a roll of 1D6 for the ability to work. The effect covers the whole board. Roll once per turn during the Power Phase, immediately after determining the Wizard's Power. If successful, the Wizard loses all his Power for that turn.

Some Monsters carry Magic Items, just like the magic items that the Warriors possess. In the Monsters' case though these items are cursed and are useless to the Warriors. If the Monster's entry indicates that it carries a magic item roll 1D6 on the following table to determine what it is. A group of the same type of Monsters have the same magic items, so you should roll for the group as a whole. If a Monster's entry indicates it gets multiple items, each of the Monsters in the group gets the same set of items, re-rolling duplicate items. If an item has an offensive capability it makes an extra attack during the Monsters' Phase. The effects of all these items cannot be dispelled. 1 Ring of Resistance. This ring gives the wearer Magic Resistance 6+. If the wearer already has the Magic Resistance special ability it improves his Magic Resistance Rating by +1. 2 Cloak of Shadows. All Warriors attempting to hit the wearer of this cloak are at -1 to their to hit rolls. 3 Warpstone Talisman. Whenever a Warrior rolls a natural 1 on his to hit roll against the wearer of this talisman the blow rebounds at full effect and he hits himself instead. 4 Doomfire Ring. The wearer may use this ring to fire a bolt of power at one of the Warriors each turn. Draw a Warrior counter to determine who is targeted. Then make a roll to hit with a Ballistic Skill of 4+. If the attack hits, the target suffers 2D6+(2 x the Dungeon level) Wounds, with no modifier for armour. 5 Mask of Radon. This mask gives the wearer Fear (Dungeon Level +3). If the wearer already causes Fear it gives him Terror (his Fear Value). 6 Collar of Vengeance. Any Warrior adjacent to the wearer at the end of the turn suffers 1D6 Wounds with no modifiers for Toughness or armour. This is Fatal Damage.

Some Monsters are naturally able to shrug off the effects of a spell cast against them. Such Monsters are said to have Magic Resistance. A Monster with this ability has a Magic Resistance Rating (n+) that is the number required on a roll of 1D6 for the protection to work. This roll is the Monster's Resistance roll. A separate Resistance roll should be made for each spell cast against the Monster. If the roll succeeds the effects of the spell may be ignored by that particular Monster. If more than one Monster with this ability can be affected by the spell, roll for each Monster individually. Note that Magic Resistance only protects the Monster from spells, not from blows by magical weapons and the like. Note that some Monsters' Magic Resistance is not an innate ability, but is due to an item or charm they wear or carry. Where this is the case, the Magic Resistance entry is suffixed with the item that provides the protection.

A Lord of Change is an extremely powerful Sorcerer and unconsciously acts as a source of power for other spellcasters. While there is a Lord of Change on the board, the Wizard doubles the result of his Power roll each turn on anything other than a roll of 1. If he rolls a l, the Wizard gets 1 point of Power, as normal.

Some Monsters bear a Magic Weapon, just like the magic weapons that the Warriors possess. In the Monsters' case though these items are cursed and will destroy any Warrior who tries to use them. If the Monster's entry indicates that it carries a magic weapon roll 1D6 on the following table to determine what it is. A group of the same type of Monsters have the same magic weapons, so you should roll for the group as a whole. 1 Cutting Edge Sword. This sword causes (+1 Wounds x the bearer's level). A Monster rolled on the Level 3 Monster Table, for instance, inflicts an extra +3 Wounds. 2 Sword of Distortion. This sword causes the wielder's outline to shimmer, making him harder to hit. Any Warrior attempting to attack a Monster hearing this sword deducts -1 from his to hit rolls. In addition, the warping nature of this sword reduces the effect of any blows that actually strike the Monster, effectively giving it +1 Toughness. 3 Sword of Pain. This sword ignores any non-magical armour and up to 3 points of magical armour when determining damage. 4 Cursed Blackblade. This sword warps time around the immediate vicinity of its wielder. Once per turn you may re-roll the bearer's first attack that misses. In addition, this sword causes an extra +lD6 Wounds. 5 Sword of Insanity. This sword gives the bearer a Fear Value equal to twice the Battle-level of the Warrior he is fighting. If the Monster wielding this sword already causes Fear it gives him a Terror Value equal to its original Fear Value. 6 Blade of the Damned. This blade causes double damage every time it strikes. (Roll the normal amount of damage, multiply the result by 2 and then add the wielder's Str.)

When an Orc or Goblin Monster has magic armour, use the following Orc Magic Armour table. 1 Armour of Gork: attackers and shooters suffer -1 to hit the wearer. 2 Armour of Magrot: when a Warrior hits, roll 1D6; on 1 the hit has no effect and the Warrior suffers 1D6 Wounds from the discharge. 3 Gork's Big Belt: at the start of any Monsters' Phase the wearer heals 1D6 Wounds while it has at least 1 Wound remaining. 4 Animated Armour of Iron Rock Fortress: each time a hit is scored, roll 1D6; on 6 the armour throws the Monster aside and the hit misses. 5 Helm of Gunbad: no normal fired or thrown weapon can hit the wearer; magic missiles are also turned aside on 5 or 6 on 1D6. 6 Mork's 'Ead: whenever any hit is scored against the wearer, roll 1D6; on 6 the attack is turned aside, and if the attack came from a non-magical weapon that weapon is destroyed.

When an Orc or Goblin Monster has a magic item, use the following Orc Magic Items table. 1 Gluvvz of Deff: the Monster gains +2 Strength and +1 Attack each turn. 2 Da Speedstone of Skabnoze: the Monster makes 1D6 Attacks per turn, whatever its profile says. 3 Armband of Agrog the Slayer: the wearer gains +1D6 Toughness against any form of attack. 4 Da Burning Brew of Peak Pass: the first time the Monster is reduced to 0 Wounds it drinks the potion, regains 2D6 Wounds, and then gains +1 Attack each turn and +2 Toughness until killed. 5 Ring of Stomping: at the start of the Monsters' Phase roll 1D6; on 6 an enormous Orc foot stomps a random Warrior for 1D6 Wounds per battle-level, with normal modifiers. 6 Grimfang's Deff Curse: when the Monster is reduced to 0 Wounds, the ring explodes and the Warrior who killed it immediately suffers 2D6 Wounds with no modifiers.

When an Orc or Goblin Monster has a magic weapon, use the following Orc Magic Weapons table. 1 Axe of Slasha: if the wielder rolls 4+ to hit, the axe causes an extra 1D6 damage. 2 Hammer of Mork: the hammer adds +1D6 to the wielder's Strength. 3 Sword of Stabbin': the sword ignores all armour, and any hit caused with it is modified for natural Toughness only. 4 Bloodletting Sword: every hit causes an extra 1D6 Wounds, but a Warrior who rolls 1 to hit suffers 1D3 Wounds with no armour or Toughness modifiers. 5 Frowing Nife: once per combat it may be thrown at a visible non-adjacent target, hits on 2+, causes 2D6 + Strength damage, ignores armour and Toughness, and may be retrieved afterwards. 6 A Dead 'Ard Weapon: roll for the weapon found, then roll for permanent abilities such as extra Wounds, armour penetration, +1 to hit, +1 Attacks, parry on 6, or +6 Wounds on a natural 6 to hit, gaining more abilities while each new roll is higher than the last.

Some Monsters have the ability to use Magic, although the spells available to them depend on their race. The descriptions of these Monsters in the Bestiary contains the spells they use. Monsters with this ability also have a Magic Rating (n) that indicates how many spells they can cast per turn. A Chaos Dwarf Sorcerer with Chaos Dwarf Magic 3, for instance, can cast 3 spells each turn from those spells available to him. Monsters with spell-casting ability do not use Power in the same way as the Wizard. Magic-using Monsters are placed on the board as if they were using missile weapons and, unless otherwise stated, are always placed first. In addition, all magic-using Monsters protect themselves with minor spells that make them harder to hit from a distance. All missile weapon attacks made against magic-using Monsters are at -1 on the to hit roll.

During the Monsters' Phase, in addition to making its normal attacks and after it has moved, a Manticore may make a special poisonous Sting attack. Each Warrior adjacent to the Manticore is automatically hit and suffers 3D6 Wounds, modified for armour and Toughness. As this attack is poisonous. all the rules for the Poison Special Ability also apply.

At the start of each Monsters' Phase, a Necromancer may cast one or more of the following spells, depending on his Magic Rating. Roll the relevant number of D6 to determine which spells are cast: 1 Winds of Death. Reduces all of the Warriors' Attacks by -1 for the next Warriors' Phase. 2 Soul Drain. Inflicts 2D6 Wounds, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour, on a single Warrior. Use the Warrior counters to determine which Warrior is affected. 3 Deathchill. Reduces a single Warrior's Toughness by -2 . If this reduces his Toughness to zero he is killed and may not he healed by any means except spells and magic items, etc., that can resurrect the dead. Use the Warrior counters to determine which Warrior is affected. If the Necromancer and all of his followers are destroyed, any Warriors whose Toughness has been reduced in this combat and are still alive have the damage to their Toughness restored. 4 Summon Skeletons. The Necromancer summons 6 Skeletons. They are placed on the board immediately and may move and fight this turn. 5 Summon Ghouls. The Necromancer summons 6 Ghouls. They are placed on the board immediately and may move and fight this turn. 6 Summon Mummies. The Necromancer summons 1D6 Mummies. They are placed on the board immediately and may move and fight this turn.

Roll to hit as normal. Any Warrior hit by a net quickly becomes enmeshed. Once a Warrior has been netted by a successful to hit roll, roll 1D6 for each additional net-armed Monster attacking him who has not yet attacked him this turn but was about to do so. On a score of 1, 2 or 3 the Warrior automatically becomes ensnared by that Monster's net as well. On a score of 4, 5 or 6 the Monster instead attacks the Warrior with a club at +2 to hit, inflicting (1D6 + Strength) Wounds if he succeeds. While netted, a Warrior may not attack in any way, including magic. At the start of each turn, roll 1D6 for each netted Warrior. add his Strength to the score and subtract the number of nets ensnaring him. If the score is 7 or more, he breaks free from all of the nets and may fight as normal.

Some Monsters are Never Pinned and may move around in combat as they please. If the Monsters in question are missile troops, each turn they will try to move to a location which allows them to take a shot. Draw a Warrior counter for each Monster to determine which Warrior it targets. If the Monsters are armed with hand-to-hand weapons, draw a Warrior counter each turn for each of them to determine which Warriors they move towards and attack.

An Orc Shaman may cast spells at the start of each Monsters' Phase. Roll 2D6 on the following table to determine which spells are cast: 2 Failure. The Shaman fails to cast a spell. 3 'Ere We Go. The Shaman floods his allies with power. All Orcs and Goblins on the board get fl Attack this turn and +1 Toughness next turn. 4 Waaagh! Roll 2D6 for each Warrior spellcaster on the board. If the score is equal to or greater than the Warrior's Battle-level he is knocked to the ground for 1 turn, during which time he may do nothing. 5 Brain Bursta. A bolt of power erupts from the Shaman's head. Draw a Warrior counter to determine which Warrior is targeted. Roll 2D6. If the score is greater than the target's Battle-level he is hit - roll 1D6: 1 The Warrior's head explodes, killing him instantly 2-5 The Warrior suffers (2 x the Dungeon level) Wounds, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour. 6 The Warrior suffers 1D6 Wounds, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour. 6 Hand of Gork. This spell moves the Shaman and any allied Monsters using missile weapons out of hand-to-hand combat with the Warriors. Move the Monsters so that they are no longer adjacent to the Warriors, moving other Monsters to make room if necessary. If the Shaman is not in hand-to-hand combat re-roll this result. 7 Fist of Gork. The Shaman's arms become enwrapped with glowing bands of power. Draw a Warrior counter to determine which Warrior is targeted. That Warrior suffers 1D6 hits at the Shaman's Strength+4. 8 Da Krunch. One of the Warriors is stamped on by the mighty Foot of Cork. Draw a Warrior counter to determine which Warrior is targeted. That Warrior suffers 1D6+10 Wounds. 9 Mork Save Uz! Roll 1D6 for each spellcasting Warrior. On a score of 4, 5 or 6 all of that Warriors' remaining points of Power are lost this turn. This does not affect any innate stored Power. 10-12 'Eadbutt. The Shaman magically 'eadbutts one of the Warrior spellcasters. If there is more than one spellcaster, draw a Warrior counter to determine which Warrior is targeted. Roll (1D6+ Dungeon level) for the Shaman and (1D6+ Battle level) for the target. If the Shaman wins or it is a draw add the two dice together: the target takes that many Wounds, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour, and cannot cast any magic next turn. If the Warrior wins the spell has no effect.

In addition to causing normal damage when they attack, some Monsters cause Paralysis. At the end of each turn in which your Warrior is wounded by such a Monster deduct 1 from his Movement. If a Warrior is reduced to 0 Movement he is killed and is removed from play. At the start each turn after that in which your Warrior was first struck roll 1D6 and add his Strength. If the score is 8 or greater the paralysis wears off and his Movement returns to its starting value. If the Warrior survives the combat, once there are no Monsters left on the board the paralysis wears off automatically and his Movement returns to normal. Paralysis can also be cured by a healing spell or healing potion, but not by bandages or provisions, or the like.

Some Monsters can Parry a Warrior's hand-to-hand attacks so that they don't cause any damage. Each time such a Monster is attacked roll 1D6. If the score is n or above, where n is the Parry Value after the Parry entry, the Monster turns the blow and it causes no damage.

Some Monsters can Petrify their foes in combat, turning them to stone with a single gaze. Any Warrior that attacks such a Monster is at -2 to his to hit rolls as he is so busy trying to avoid its gaze. In addition, if a Warrior attacking a Monster with a Petrify attack scores a natural 1 on his to hit roll he has been caught by the creature's gaze and starts to turn to stone. He immediately suffers 3D6 Wounds, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour, and for the rest of the adventure he is at -1 Movement. If his Movement is subsequently reduced to 0 by any means while in the dungeon he is immediately turned to stone, beyond all reach of healing magic. All Monsters with a Petrify attack also have natural Magic Resistance 4+ and, if their Resistance Roll is a natural 6, may immediately turn their Petrify attack on the spellcaster that attacked them and inflict the effects given above.

Some Monsters carry a horrible, disfiguring disease known simply as the Plague and may infect their opponents with it in combat. If your Warrior is reduced to 0 Wounds by such a Monster his Toughness is permanently reduced by -1 when he is healed to 1 or more Wounds. If a Warrior is reduced to 0 Toughness he is killed and removed from play.

Some Monsters have Poison attacks. If a Warrior is reduced to 0 Wounds by a Poison attack his Strength is permanently reduced by -1 when he is healed to 1 or more Wounds. If a Warrior is reduced to 0 Strength he is killed and removed from play.

A Lord of Change has the ability to Dispel Magic in the surrounding area, just like some other Monsters, but to a much greater degree. This ability works exactly like the normal Magic Dispel in that the Lord of Change can actively interfere with and nullify any spell cast on the board, not just those cast directly against it. When dispelling magic cast against the Lord of Change's minions the spell is dispelled on a 1D6 roll of 4 or 5, and deflected back against the caster on a roll of 6. Furthermore, when dispelling beneficial magic cast by the Warriors against themselves the spell is dispelled on a 1D6 roll of 4 or 5, and affects the Lord of Change on a roll of 6. A Lord of Change also has Magic Resistance just like some other Monsters, but to a much greater degree. The Lord of Change has a Magic Resistance Rating of 4+. In addition, if the Resistance roll scores a 5 or 6 the spell is deflected rather than simply resisted and affects the Warrior who cast it instead. Note that Magic Resistance only protects of the Lord of Change from spells, not from blows by magical weapons and the like.
Some Monsters wear magical rings that give them an extra +n Toughness, where n is the number in the brackets following the Protection Ring entry.

Some Monsters can Regenerate Wounds while they remain alive, making them much harder to kill. Each Monster with this ability has a Regeneration Rating (n+) that is the number of D6 Wounds that the Monster regains at the end of each turn. A Monster with Regenerate 2, for instance, regains 2D6 Wounds at the end of each turn. A Monster cannot regenerate if it is reduced to 0 Wounds or fewer and can never have more Wounds than it started with (except Vampires).

When a mounted Monster attacks, both rider and mount may attack in the Monsters' Phase. Rider and mount must attack the same Warrior. A Warrior can't attack the rider until its mount is dead. When the mount has been killed, the rider will continue to fight on foot. Either make a note that the mount has been killed, or remove the mounted model and replace it with an appropriate model to represent the rider. If a Warrior kills a mount with one blow, he gets a death-blow as normal. However, the death-blow cuts into the rider first. If the death-blow kills the rider as well, it moves onto the next target.
Roll 1D6 and subtract this number from the Power Phase roll while this model is alive.

Skaven Warpfire Thrower teams are placed as if they were armed with missile weapons. The warpfire throwers that they carry consist of a tank of superheated warpstone connected to a gun mechanism that can spray the tainted flame across a wide area. Each warpfire thrower requires a team of two Skaven to use it. If one of the Skaven is killed the other cannot continue to use the weapon and may only make a normal hand-to-hand attack from then on. At the start of the Monsters' Phase draw a Warrior counter to determine which Warrior is targeted by the team. If there is more than one team in combat, distribute their hits according to the normal One-on-one rule. Each time a warpfire thrower is fired, the flames form a 1-square wide corridor that travels across the room in a straight line toward the target, hitting everything in its path. If a model is hit by a warpfire thrower, it suffers 3D6 Wounds. If the dice come up with 2 or 3 natural 1's the warpfire thrower has misfired and you should roll on the following table: 1-4 Kaboom! The warpfire thrower explodes, causing 4D6 Wounds on every model in the room. Roll 1D6 for each additional warpfire thrower in the room. On a score of 1, 2 or 3 it too blows up, causing the same damage and requiring you to make another 1D6 roll for each intact warpfire thrower, and so on. 5-6 Click-whoosh! The warpfire thrower backfires, causing 3D6 Wounds against its crew. In addition, roll 1D6 for each item of magical treasure the victim is carrying. On a roll of 1 the item is rendered useless for the rest of the dungeon. At the start of the next dungeon the item may be used normally again.
Skaven jezzails are missile weapons and Skaven that carry them are placed as such. The jezzail is a Strength 5 weapon that ignores up to 3 points of the victim's armour when determining damage.

A Skaven plague censer is a heavy spiked ball on a length of chain. Make a normal hand-to-hand to hit roil against the target to determine if he is struck. If the target is hit, the censer causes iD6fStrength damage, as usual, plus the victim must make a Toughness check. Roll 1D6 and add the victim's Toughness. If the score is 7 or more he is unaffected by the fumes that surround the censer. If the score is equal to or less than 7 he takes an additional 1D3 Wounds, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour.

Skaven Poison Globadiers are missile troops and are treated just as if they were using normal missile weapons, applying all the normal missile weapon rules that implies. Work out which Warriors have been hit. The poison globe attack affects those Warriors and all Warriors adjacent to them. Each affected Warrior rolls 1D6 and adds their Toughness to their score. If the total is 6 or less, the Warrior suffers 1D3 Wounds, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour. However, as the globes they carry are poisoned, in addition to suffering normal damage the victim of a globe attack suffers all the effects of the Poison Special Ability. To summarise: if a Warrior is reduced to 0 Wounds by a Poison Globe his Strength is permanently reduced by -1. If a Warrior is reduced to 0 Strength he is killed and is removed from play.

At the start of each Monsters' Phase, a Skaven spellcaster may cast one or more of the following spells. Roll the relevant number of 1D6 on the following table to determine which spells are cast: 1 Warp Power. The Skaven uses the power of the warp to heal himself, curing 2D6 of his Wounds up to a maximum of his Starting Wounds. If the Skaven is already at his Starting Wounds score re-roll this result. 2 Pestilent Breath. The Skaven opens its jaws and black, pestilential fumes pour out and flood into the dungeon. Roll 1D6 for each Warrior and add his Toughness to the score. If the score is greater than 7 he is not affected. If the score is equal to or less than 7 he suffers (1D3 x the dungeon level) Wounds, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour. 3 Warp Lightning. Screeching in fury, the Skaven points its clawed hand at one of the Warriors and then blasts him with a streak of warp lightning. Draw a Warrior counter to determine who is targeted. The bolt causes (2D6 + the Dungeon level) Wounds, with no modifier for armour. 4 Wither. A greenish pallid glow spreads from the Skaven's outstretched paw. All adjacent Warrior suffer (1D6 + the Dungeon level) Wounds, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour. If there are no Warriors adjacent to the Skaven re-roll this result. 5 Putrefy. The Warriors see their surroundings begin to rot and decay, with worms and maggots writhing upon the bodies of the dead and wounded. The hallucination created by the Skaven is terrifyingly real. Roll 1D6 for each Warrior. On a score of 5 or 6 that Warrior is unaffected by the spell. On a score of 1, 2, 3 or 4 that Warrior may do nothing at all in the next Warriors' Phase. 6 Scorch. Flames engulf one of the Warriors, turning him into an incandescent column of fire. Draw a Warrior counter to determine who is targeted. The flames cause 4D6 Wounds, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour.

This ability allows the Vermin Lord to disappear, move through the warp and then reappear next to its chosen victim. By moving in this way the Vermin Lord is not subject to pinning and may move through any obstacles as though they were not there. If the Vermin Lord manages to kill a Warrior and still has some Attacks left he may switch to another target to use them.

Beasts of Nurgle secrete a Slime Trail around them as they move. Any Warrior who finishes his turn in a square adjacent to a Beast of Nurgle suffers 1 Wound, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour, as the poisonous gunk attacks his flesh.
A Lammasu breathes out whirling tendrils of magic that provide protection against magical attacks. All attacks made with a magical weapon against a Lammasu have a -2 modifier on the to hit roll.

Some Monsters may Sting their opponents, but only if all of their normal attacks hit in a single turn. The sting does the damage indicated in the brackets after the Sting entry. Sting attacks are also subject to the rules for Poison attacks, above.

Great Unclean Ones have a special type of attack where they vomit a foul smelling mass of pus over their foes. This is called a Stream of Corruption. A Great Unclean One may use this attack once per turn, at the start of the Monsters' Phase. Any Warrior on the same board section as the Great Unclean One may be attacked with the Stream of Corruption. Draw a Warrior counter to determine which Warrior is targeted. Draw a line from the centre of the Great Unclean One's Square to the target square: every square that this line passes through is affected by the Stream of Corruption (see the diagram). Roll 2D6 for each Warrior standing in a targeted square. If the score is equal to or less than his Initiative he manages to dodge the Stream and suffers no ill effects. If the score is greater than the Warrior's Initiative he suffers 3D6 Wounds, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour. If a Warrior is reduced to 0 Wounds by the Stream of Corruption his Toughness is permanently reduced by -1. If a Warrior is reduced to 0 Toughness he is killed and is removed from play.

Some Monsters are painted with complicated protective Tattoos that act like armour. Whether this is due to some arcane power inherent in the tattoos, or simply the strength of belief on the wearer's part, they do seem to work. Each time a Monster wearing tattoos is hit roll 1D6. If the score is n or more, where n is the number after the Tattoos entry, the blow has no effect. A Monster with Tattoos 5+, for instance, ignores incoming blows on a 1D6 roll of 5 or 6.

Some Monsters cause Terror. This is similar to Fear, only much, much worse. Each Monster that causes Terror has a Terror Value, indicated by the n after the Terror entry. When an event occurs that generates Monsters that cause Terror, roll 1D6+(your Warrior's Battle-level) for each Warrior as soon as the Monsters are placed on the board. This is the Warrior's Terror roll. If the total is greater than the Monsters' Terror Value, that Warrior is not afraid of them during this combat and may fight them as normal. If the total is less than or equal to the Monsters' Terror Value, that Warrior is terrified of them during this combat, and fights them at -2 on his to hit rolls. If a Wizard fails his Terror roll any spells he attempts to cast against that Monster have their Casting Numbers increased by +2. Each time the Warriors meet a particular type of Monster that causes Terror they must make a Terror roll, as the effect of the roll, whether good or bad, applies only to that type of Monster during this one combat.

Some Monsters are armed with weapons that they can Throw, such a spears. As soon as such Monsters are placed on the board, before the Warriors can do anything, they throw these weapons in a special, single attack. You need to roll against the Monster's Ballistic Skill to see if they hit, just as with any other missile weapon. The thrown weapon has Strength n, where n is the value in the brackets after the Throw entry. The weapon therefore causes (the Monster's damage dice)+n Wounds, modified for the target's Toughness and armour, according to the normal rules for missile weapons. A Beastman Lord with a Strength 10 spear, for instance, causes 3D6+10 Wounds, modified for Toughness and armour. After throwing their special weapons these Monsters attack in hand-to-hand combat in the Monsters' Phase as normal.

Some Monsters cause Tomb Rot, their mere presence rotting nearby creature's flesh. Any Warrior who ends the turn adjacent to such a Monster takes an extra 1Dn Wounds (where n is the number in brackets after the Tomb Rot entry), with no modifiers for Toughness or armour. Tomb Rot is Fatal Damage. A Warrior standing next to two Mummies, for instance, takes 2D3 Wounds at the very end of each turn.

Dragons have their own treasure hoards above and beyond the treasure that is otherwise found. When a dragon is killed roll 1D6+n, where n is the number after the Treasure Hoard entry, on the following table to determine what it contains: 1 Gold. Each player may roll as many D6 as he likes, adding up the score and multiplying it by 10 to determine how much gold his Warrior finds. However, if any of the dice score a 1 his Warrior finds nothing. 2-3 Dungeon Room Treasure. Make one roll on the Dungeon Room Treasure Table for each Warrior to determine what he finds. 4-5 Dungeon Room Treasure and Gold. Make one roll on the Dungeon Room Treasure Table for each Warrior to determine what he finds. In addition, each player may roll as many D6 as he likes, adding up the score and multiplying it by 10 to determine how much extra gold his Warrior finds. However, if any of the dice score a 1 his Warrior finds no extra gold. 6+ Objective Room Treasure. Make one roll on the Objective Room Treasure Table for each Warrior to determine what he finds.

In addition to their hand-to-hand attacks, Vampires may make a single special attack each turn. At the start of the Monsters' Phase roll 1D6 on the following table to determine what the Vampire does this turn. 1 Transfix. The Vampire transfixes one of the Warriors with his steely glare. Draw a Warrior counter to determine who is affected. Roll 1D6 and add that Warrior's Initiative. If the score is 11 or less he is transfixed for 1 turn. While transfixed the Warrior may do absolutely nothing and any Monster in combat with him automatically hits. 2 Regenerate. The Vampire regains 3D6 Wounds at the end of this turn, even if this takes him above his Starting Wounds score. The Vampire cannot regenerate if it is killed. 3 Bite. The Vampire bites all adjacent Warriors. Each suffers (the Dungeon level) Wounds, with no deductions for Toughness or armour. The Vampire then gains the total number of Wounds caused, adding them to his own Wounds, even if this takes him above his Starting Wounds score. 4 Ethereal. The Vampire become Ethereal 2 until the start of the next Monsters' Phase. For the duration of this state he has a Chill 3 attack rather than his normal hand-to-hand attacks. 5 Cloak of Darkness. The Vampire gathers a mantle of blackness around him. Until the start of the next Monsters' Phase all attacks against him are made at -1 to hit. In addition, all Warriors on the same board section as the Vampire lose 1 Attack in the next Warriors' Phase. 6 Turns to Smoke. At the end of the Monsters' Phase, after making any hand-to-hand attack, the Vampire vanishes in a pall of smoke. Remove the model from the board. At the start of the next Monsters' Phase take a Warrior counter and place the Vampire back on the board next to that Warrior. In addition, if the Vampire left the board on less than full Wounds he returns fully healed and back up to his Starting Wounds score.

Some Monsters (namely Trolls) have a special Vomit attack, regurgitating the highly corrosive contents of its stomach all over the Warrior it is attacking. In addition to making such a Monster's normal attacks, roll 1D6. If the score is a 5 or 6, the Monster has vomited on its foe. If the victim has any armour (including shields or a helmet) he loses one piece of it as the acid eats it away. The player concerned may choose which item his Warrior loses. If he hasn't got any armour, the target loses 1D6+2 Wounds, with no deductions for Toughness.

Certain Monsters (mainly Spiders) attempt to ensnare their prey in a Web, so that they can finish it off at their leisure. Each turn a Monster may attempt to web a Warrior as many times as it has Attacks. If the Monster hits your Warrior he has been caught in its web - place a Webbed counter next to the Warrior. While webbed a Warrior may not do anything. While webbed, a Warrior may be bitten automatically with the rest of the Monster's Attacks (no to hit roll is needed) for 1Dn Wounds per bite (where n is the value after the Web entry), with no modifiers for Toughness or armour. A Monster may not use its bite Attack(s) if its opponent has not been webbed. Monsters without the ability to use webs that may also be involved in the combat get +2 to their to hit rolls against a webbed Warrior. At the start of each Warriors' Phase, roll 1D6 for each Warrior that has been webbed and add his Strength. If the total is 7 or more, he pulls himself free from the sticky strands and may act normally this turn.

Weeping Blades are poisonous weapons. If a Warrior is reduced to 0 Wounds by a Poison attack his Strength is permanently reduced by -1 when he is healed to 1 or more Wounds. If a Warrior is reduced to 0 Strength he is killed and removed from play.

At the start of the phase during which they appear, and each Monsters' Phase thereafter, roll 1D6 for each wild Squig. On a score of 1 the Squig falls asleep and does nothing this turn. On a score of 2, 3 or 4 it immediately attacks one of the Warriors. Draw a Warrior counter to see which Warrior is attacked. On a score of 5 or 6 the Squig bounds off and immediately attacks the nearest Monster. If there is more than one potential target use a dice to determine which Monster is attacked.
Use the default ambience for the Temple of Khaine without a specific mission from the Adventure Book.
Resolve the objective room using its normal behaviour. If you use this room without a specific mission, apply its standard special rules and the corresponding monster table.

The Warriors encounter Stephano Whitewolf, the leader of a newly organised mercenary company in Couronne known as the Golden Pillars, which consists of a motley group of adventurers. The Golden Pillars are virtuous and upright, trying to subtly direct immoral lords and foil the plans of the wicked. To gain admission into the company and prove their worth, it has been decided that the Warriors must eradicate the growing presence of a Cult of Slaanesh. The faction has recently taken up residence in the sea caves north of Couronne and Stephano wants to remove them before they become a nuisance. The Warriors are given a Golden Pillar, a banner emblazoned with the group's gold emblem, and are told to place it inside the cult's temple in order to complete their membership. Each Warrior should roll 1D6, the one with the highest score being selected to carry the banner. Monsters are at -1 To Hit the Warrior who carries the banner due to its fearsome markings and bold colors. If a Warrior carrying the banner dies, the other Warriors may roll off again to see who gets to pick it up. The whole initiation is merely a formality. Stephano expects the Warriors to simply enter the caverns, run off a few cult members, plant the flag, and return to a hero's welcome. As Stephano figures it, the more members that exist, the more influence his mercenary company will possess, so he always assigns a fairly simple task to newcomers. Unbeknownst to the Warriors and Stephano, the caves have already been cleansed of these cult members by the Hag Queen Melusina and her Dark Elf clan. Her Black Ark raids have been instrumental in maintaining a supply of fresh blood for her magical cauldron, which in turn bestows upon her eternal beauty. Presently, the Black Ark has returned to Naggaroth with slaves and sacrifices, but Melusina and a Dark Elf raiding party remain to terrorise the coast.

For this Adventure, shuffle the Circle of Power card into the top half of the Dungeon Deck. This Adventure uses the special Hall of the Hag Queen counters. There are books of interest located in the Bottomless Pit, the Water Cave, and the Circle of Power. Each book tells a bit of history, legend, and lore about the Dark Elves, Black Arks, the Cauldron of Blood, and the Hag Queens. For each Book that turns out to be important, you may add +1 to the dice roll when rolling on the Objective Room Monster Table. If there are no Monsters on one of those Board Sections and the party leader is standing on a square that contains a Book, they may pick it up to see if it contains anything important. Take the corresponding Book Counter and the three Skull Counters and put all four of them into a cup. Mix them up and have the party leader pick one from the cup without looking. If it is the Book Counter, put it on the Adventure Record Sheet. If the Warriors draw a Skull Counter instead, the book is unimportant and also trapped, so they must immediately draw an Event Card. Repeat this process for each relevant Board Section. Once the Objective Room is clear of Monsters, a secret passage opens on the wall opposite the entrance. The Warriors plant the Golden Pillar and can leave the room by this exit, which leads up to the surface. Stephano Whitewolf welcomes them into the organisation with an honorary gift from the company vaults, one item of Dungeon Room Treasure to distribute as normal. Bretonnia is also glad to have the Dark Elf presence removed and the King rewards the Warriors with 1D6 x 100 gold each.

The town of St. Leu is refusing to pay taxes because they do not feel their monies are at work protecting them after the third Dark Elf raid in a month, the last of which occurred just before the Warriors arrived. The townsfolk are furious and have begun to steer travellers and merchants away from the area in protest. In a desperate effort to quell the fears of the citizens, the Burgermeister hires the Warriors as mercenaries to pursue the Dark Elf raiders. The Warriors track the Dark Elves to a cavern system on the coast which they bravely enter.

Take the Torture Chamber Dungeon Room card and shuffle it into the top half of the Dungeon Deck. Shuffle the Temple of Khaine card into the bottom half of the Dungeon Deck for a total of 14 cards. Remove the Execution Event Card from the deck for this Adventure. Instead of drawing an Event Card when the Warriors enter the Torture Chamber, they encounter Executioner Husk Bawn. In addition, draw Event Cards until one reveals Monsters, reshuffling any E Events back into the deck, to see who accompanies him. If the Warriors clear the Board of Monsters there, each Warrior receives one item of Dungeon Room Treasure instead of a single item for the group. If the Warriors find the Temple of Khaine Objective Room and defeat all the Monsters, they discover a false panel in the wall that leads out of the Dungeon and back to the surface. They can return to St. Leu where they are paid 1D6 x 100 gold each by the Burgermeister. In addition, the townsfolk are so relieved that the Dark Elf raids have been stopped that some of the houses donate a family heirloom, an item of Dungeon Room Treasure, to each Warrior.

Strolling about the streets of Hargendorf in-between adventures, the Warriors inadvertently insult an old wretch begging in the streets. She gives them an evil stare and curses them in a foreign tongue before shuffling off. Not soon afterward bad luck follows the Warriors like an animal stalking its prey. The Warriors have no choice but to seek out the old woman, whom they find in a craggy cave overlooking the sea. As if expecting them, she welcomes them into her hovel, declaring that she will remove the hex if the Warriors bring her the blood of a Dark Elf Hag Queen. Following a pointing finger out toward the ocean, the Warriors can see the vague outline of a dark fortress floating on the water. The Hag Queen awaits deep within the cavernous holds of the foreboding vessel.

Until the hex is removed, any Warrior who rerolls any dice that successfully alters consequences in their favour must make a Hex Roll immediately afterward by rolling 1D6. This includes Luck Points, Faith Points, the Wizard's Staff, and rerolling dice due to Magic Items, Magic Weapons, Skills, and similar effects. If the result of the Hex Roll is a 1 or 2, the old woman's curse negates the roll as if it never happened. This Hex Roll cannot be rerolled by any means. When the Warriors enter the Objective Room, the Hag Queen Melusina and the old wretch that hexed them in Hargendorf are revealed to be one and the same. Because she was expecting the Warriors, subtract 1 when the Warriors roll on the Dark Elves Objective Room Monster Table. If the Warriors manage to clear the Objective Room, a secret recess opens on the cavern wall opposite the entrance. The small chamber contains captured treasure from various Dark Elf raids as well as a passage to the surface. As the Warriors pass through the doorway, they may each take one random item of Objective Room Treasure before making their way back to shore.

It had been days since the Warriors had slept and the weary party spent the night in a grove just a few miles from Bordeleaux. They awoke in chains, being whipped and beaten, and forced to march through a dank underground tunnel by their Dark Elf captors. An encounter with trolls allowed them to break their bonds and escape. They have now made tracks back to Bordeleaux where they plan bloody retribution.

Take the Dungeon Cell Dungeon Room card and shuffle it in with five other cards to make the top half of the Dungeon Deck. Shuffle the Water Cave Dungeon Room and the Temple of Khaine Objective Room in with five other cards to make the bottom half of the Dungeon Deck. The Warriors begin the Adventure stripped of all of their Weapons, Armour, gold and other items, although they have each managed to locate one hand-to-hand weapon of their choice. They may choose a sword, axe, spear, mace, warhammer, flail or halberd to begin this Adventure. If the Warriors defeat the first Monsters they encounter, they find their lost Weapons. If the Warriors find the Dungeon Cell and clear the Board of Monsters, they find their lost Armour. If the Warriors find the Water Cave and clear the Board of Monsters, they find their lost Gold. If the Warriors find the Temple of Khaine Objective Room and defeat all the Monsters inside, they will find the rest of their missing items. If the Warriors discover the Temple of Khaine before the Water Cave, place the second Doorway on the Temple of Khaine so that the Warriors can continue their search for their gold. Place the three Skull Counters and one of the Book Counters into a cup. Each Warrior receives +1 Attack per turn while their wrath endures. Each time they reveal a new Board Section in the Exploration Phase, each Warrior, starting with the Party Leader, must draw a counter from the cup and return it before the next Warrior draws. If a Warrior draws a Skull Counter, their wrath remains at its peak and they continue to receive +1 Attack per turn. If a Warrior draws a Book Counter instead, their wrath has waned and they no longer receive the bonus. The last Board Section in the Dungeon Deck leads out of the Dungeon and up to the surface. The Warriors have completed their quest and can bask in the satisfaction of revenge.

The Bretonnian King's nephew was among the latest victims taken away in a Black Ark raid on the coast of Brionne. The King has demanded that the Warriors rescue his nephew. Although there is no conceivable way to penetrate the magical barrier that surrounds the Black Ark as it sails back to Naggaroth, the King's advisors have devised a desperate scheme: after the royal Enchantress renders the Warriors' weapons invisible by way of a magic spell, the Warriors will allow themselves to be captured by a Dark Elf raiding party. Once inside and after the invisibility spell wears off, they are expected to locate the royal nephew and lead him safely back to Bretonnia. The Warriors see that their best chance of surviving the whole ordeal is to ignite a slave revolt.

Prepare the Dungeon Deck as normal with one exception: shuffle the Fighting Pit Objective Room into the top seven cards and the Temple of Khaine Objective Room into the bottom four cards to form a total of 13 cards for the Dungeon Deck. Put the Demona Ryme Beastmaster Event Card aside for now. The first Board Section is the Warriors' dungeon cell where the invisibility spell wears off. Draw an Event Card until it reveals Monsters, reshuffling any E Event Cards back into the deck. The Warriors receive the equivalent of an Ambush attack on their astonished captors, taking an extra Warriors' Phase in the first round of combat. If the Warriors defeat their captors they receive one item of Dungeon Room Treasure as normal, and there is only one exit in this room. When the Warriors enter the Fighting Pit Objective Room they are attacked by Beastmaster Ryme, Julong the Wardog, 1 Ogre slave, 1D6 + 3 Hobgoblins slaves, and 1D6 Dark Elves with Crossbows of Strength 4. If Demona Ryme is killed before the Ogre, then the Ogre receives the Break ability. If the Warriors clear the Fighting Pit of Monsters, they can attempt to break down the huge wooden door, Toughness 3 and Wounds 7, and free the captives inside who rush out in droves. The royal nephew is not among them and has been taken to the Temple of Khaine where he will be sacrificed. When the Warriors discover the Temple of Khaine Objective Room in the Exploration Phase, roll 2D6. This is how many turns they have to kill the Hag Queen before she finishes her ritual by plunging a wicked-looking dagger into the royal nephew. Once the Warriors clear the Objective Room, they find a passage out of the Black Ark that leads to the outside world. They leap into the ocean where they are soon rescued by a passing ship. If they have saved the royal nephew, return to the King of Bretonnia and roll 1D6. On 1 to 4, the King is shocked that the Warriors actually completed the mission since it was he who had arranged for the nephew's disappearance in the first place; he grudgingly rewards the Warriors with 1D6 x 100 gold each. On 5 or 6, the King is elated and rewards each Warrior with 1D6 x 100 gold and one item of Objective Room Treasure. If they fail to save the nephew, the Warriors decide a long sabbatical is in order, away from vicious monsters, dangerous dungeons, and irrational rulers with guillotines.

It is known that the dark caves dotted along the Old World are numerous and, sometimes, enormous. It is also known that their uncharted underground waterways extend from the coast of Estalia to the coast of Kislev, emerging at hundreds of points along the way. The coastal caverns were used decades ago by pirates, smugglers, and traders to evade and circumvent the authorities. When the ocean levels rose, the caverns became all but useless. But the water levels have receded once again and adventurers know that the caves may be ripe with bandit gold and pirate treasure. The Warriors have set their sights on the Old Coast Road near Marienburg, but have come before the High Elves of Marienburg to ask permission, for the High Elves believe that they are the rightful occupants of Marienburg and the surrounding area. They insist that it is written in the Three Ancient Tomes, ancient historical texts that detail the history of their ancestors, the Sea Elves, during the time when they reigned supreme. Unfortunately for them, the texts have been missing for generations. The High Elves, impressed with the Warriors' respect and courtesy, have granted them permission to search for gold and treasure within their ancestral home, but with a single condition: the books would be very valuable to the High Elves, as they would reveal some of the missing history of their kin and may prove the High Elves' claim to the area.

There are Ancient Tomes located in the Bottomless Pit, Water Cave and Temple of Khaine Board Sections. When there are no Monsters on one of those Board Sections, the Warriors may read a tome to see if it is one they seek. The first time the Warriors find a Tome, put the corresponding Tome Counter into a cup with three Skull Counters and, without looking, have the leader draw one out. If the Tome Counter drawn matches the one on the Board, then it is one of the Three Ancient Tomes the Elves seek. The second time the Warriors find a Tome, put the corresponding Tome Counter into a cup with only two Skull Counters and draw again. When the Warriors come across the third Tome, put that Tome Counter in a cup with only one Skull Counter and draw again. When the Warriors reach the Temple of Khaine Objective Room, roll on the Dark Elves Objective Room Monster Table to see who attacks them. The last card in the Dungeon Deck contains the exit out of the Dungeon. Use the second Doorway in the Temple of Khaine Objective Room to continue through the rest of the Dungeon Deck if appropriate. The Warriors can then return to Marienburg where they deliver any treasure of Elven origin they found. As promised, the High Elves pay twice the gold value of each such treasure. In addition, for every Tome they bring back that discusses Sea Elf history, the Warriors receive one item of Objective Room Treasure, rerolling any treasure of Elven origin, to distribute as normal.

At the start of each turn roll 1D6. The result shows how many Warriors are engulfed by the oily fumes. If the score is higher than the number of Warriors present, all of them are hit. If not, use Warrior counters to determine who is affected. Roll 1D6 for each Warrior hit, subtract their Toughness from the score and multiply the result by 2. The total shows how many D6 Wounds that Warrior suffers, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour. This attack is used in addition to the dragon's normal hand-to-hand attacks.

At the start of each turn roll 1D6 for each Warrior on the same board section. If the score is 4 or more, that Warrior suffers the listed attack with no modifiers for Toughness or armour. This attack is used in addition to the dragon's normal hand-to-hand attacks.
A Black Ark Corsair wears a cloak made from Sea Dragon scales. When rolling Damage Dice against a Corsair with a Dragon Cloak, any roll of 1 or 2 is ignored as the blow is deflected.
A Cold One's hide drips with toxic slime. Any Warrior wounded in hand-to-hand combat also suffers an extra 1D3 Wounds with no modifiers for Toughness or armour.
Executioners are trained to deliver a single perfect strike. On a natural 6 to hit, the attack ignores the target's armour and Toughness.

The Monster's blades are coated in deadly toxins. When the Monster is placed, roll 1D6: on 1-3 it uses Dark Venom, on 4-6 it uses Manbane. Resolve any Wounds caused by these poisoned attacks with no healing until the end of the turn.
If Julong starts the Monsters' Phase adjacent to Demona Ryme, he may make one extra attack against the same Warrior she attacks.

After making its attacks, the Monster may melt back into the shadows. Roll 1D6. If the score is equal to or higher than the value shown, remove it from the board. At the start of the next Monsters' Phase, place it back on the board adjacent to a randomly determined Warrior.
A River Troll's skin is coated in foul mucus. Any non-magical attack that wounds it is reduced by 1D6 before Toughness and armour are applied.
This Monster carries two Magic Items. Roll twice on the standard Magic Item table, rerolling duplicates if needed.
This Monster carries three Magic Items. Roll three times on the standard Magic Item table, rerolling duplicates if needed.
Suddenly the ground hardens and, among the dunes, a graveyard of bones from an immense creature of ages past emerges.
See the Adventure Book for details about this room.
Among the desert dunes, beside some ruins, you find the coveted Fountain of Life, from which the sacred waters of Khemri flow.
See the Adventure Book for details about this room.
A cobbled path stretches before you, as if it once belonged to the streets of a forgotten city.
Roads will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The path forks and you do not know which direction to choose; the heat bears down and your supplies are running short.
Junctions will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
A stone stairway lifts you up along the path. From it, you can glimpse part of the desert.
Stairs will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The path bends and the sandy wind blinds you. Every step becomes more uncertain.
Passageways will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The path suddenly turns and the vast desert seems endless to you.
Corners will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
A junction opens into four possible directions to take.
Junctions will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
A dune track lies before you; every step sinks deep and the sand-laden wind blinds you.
Passageways will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The path suddenly turns and the vast desert seems endless to you.
Corners will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The path bends and the sandy wind blinds you. Every step becomes more uncertain.
Passageways will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
A junction appears on your route, offering new directions to take.
Junctions will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The path forks and you do not know which direction to choose; the heat bears down and your supplies are running short.
Junctions will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
Beside a small grate in the centre lie two chained bodies, consumed by the passage of time.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
Amid your exploits you come upon a water-filled pit. It seems like a good moment to rest.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
The ground hardens and, among the dunes, you find a kind of stair hidden in the desert.
Stairs will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The desert dunes become firmer than usual; a small rocky patch rises above the sand.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
The ruins of a small house that was destroyed have been overrun by the monsters of the area.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
A small oasis in the middle of nowhere offers the party a little peace and rest.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
This room is flooded with supreme magical power. Not everyone is capable of surviving it.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
Candles arranged in a circle surround a mystic symbol. A bad omen comes over you as you reach this room.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
Remnants of ancient ruins rise from the sand; it is hard to tell from which age they date.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
The bones of a prisoner lie inside the cage; you do not know whether time or the fierce heat claimed him.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
Brightly coloured tiles form a straight corridor. They look almost new, which suggests very few people have reached this place.
Roads will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The path forks and you do not know which direction to choose; the heat bears down and your supplies are running short.
Junctions will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
Stairs made of ancient tiles, still gleaming despite the passing of the years.
Stairs will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
A junction appears in the dungeon, with new directions to take.
Junctions will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
Without knowing what lies ahead, the corridor turns and forces you deeper into the dungeon.
Corners will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The corridor bends, but the darkness keeps you from seeing what lies ahead.
Passageways will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The chamber of dusk, the end of times where everything ends, also known as the deathly hall.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
The adventurers feel their Wounds mending; the tiles shine and fill those who step into the chamber with courage.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
After climbing the steps, you find a raised walkway with water on either side.
See the Adventure Book for details about this room.
A mummified body rests atop a small altar surrounded by skulls and burning candles.
See the Adventure Book for details about this room.
The passage crosses a narrow chamber with two enormous statues at its entrance.
See the Adventure Book for details about this section.
A huge carving of a scorpion lies on the floor, with a skull in each corner.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
A junction appears in the dungeon, with new directions to take.
Junctions will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The corridor turns sharply and you have no idea what lies in the depths of the dungeon.
Corners will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
An empty room, barely lit, with paving stones worn down by the passage of time.
Upon entering a Dungeon Room, draw an Event Card unless the Adventure Book states otherwise.
A faint corridor lit by torches fixed to the walls.
Passageways will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The corridor splits and you do not know which direction to choose; whichever path you take, you sense nothing good ahead.
Junctions will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
Stairs made of ancient tiles, still gleaming despite the passing of the years.
Stairs will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
The corridor bends, yet the darkness prevents you from seeing what lies ahead.
Passageways will always be empty unless the Adventure Book states otherwise or an Unexpected Event occurs during the Power Phase.
A long chamber lined with columns and, at the far end, a great throne flanked by a banner on each side.
See the Adventure Book for details about this room.
Walls of skulls form the vaulted chamber, with a great emblem engraved on the floor.
See the Adventure Book for details about this room.
A massive Orc Big'Un strides into the room and bellows, "Which wun of yooz iz gonna fight me, Skullbasha?"

Skullbasha carries the Sword of Malediction, a special blade that nullifies magic. One Warrior may fight him in single combat, or the whole party may attack him as normal. If one Warrior accepts, the duel lasts until one combatant is reduced to 0 Wounds. If Skullbasha wins, he takes one of that Warrior's Treasure cards, preferably a magic weapon, and leaves. If the Warrior wins, he may take the sword. While the duel lasts, no unexpected events occur. If any other Warrior intervenes, or if all the Warriors attack Skullbasha together, he immediately calls for help. Roll on the Minions Table, then resolve the combat normally. In that case the Warriors gain no treasure for completing the event and cannot take the Sword of Malediction because it is broken during the fight. Skullbasha only appears once per adventure; if this event is generated again, ignore it and roll again on the table. Skullbasha: Move 4, Weapon Skill 4, Strength 4, Toughness 5, Attacks 1, Gold 120. He has 10 +1 Wounds per battle-level of the challenged Warrior. His damage is 1D6+4 against Novices, 2D6+4 against Champions, 3D6+4 against Heroes and 4D6+4 against Lords.
In a niche in the wall is a small, ferocious-looking golden statue of an unfamiliar but distinctly Orcish deity.

Draw a Warrior counter to see who takes the statue, then roll 1D6. 1-2: the statue is cursed. The Warrior loses a Luck counter; if he has none left, he permanently loses 2 Wounds from his starting score, then puts the idol back. 3-4: an unseen attacker repeatedly headbutts him. He drops the idol and suffers 1D6 Wounds +1 Wound per battle-level, with no deductions. When the attack stops, the idol is back in its alcove. 5-6: the statue is real gold and worth 500 gold. Until the end of the adventure, however, Orcs, Goblins and other greenskins are not placed using the One-on-One rule. Instead, as many as possible attack the Warrior carrying the idol. If that Warrior is reduced to 0 Wounds, the statue is gone when he is healed.
Roll 1D6. On a score of 1, 2 or 3 draw another Event card immediately.
Bogoff the Snotling scuttles into view, bites the leader's hand, grabs the lantern and runs off with it.

Place Bogoff as far from the Warriors as possible while keeping them in the lantern light. At the start of each Monsters' Phase he moves away again, still trying not to leave them in darkness. He is never pinned, can move through occupied squares, dodges all hits on 4+, and even successful hits merely make him drop the lantern and flee. Bogoff is temporarily immune to magic, including automatically hitting spells. Each turn while he has the lantern, if a 1 is rolled in the Power Phase he blows it out. Every Warrior must then roll 1D6 and add Willpower. Any who score less than 7 are lost in the dark and may only defend or heal. Any who score 7 or more may move and fight normally, but suffer -2 to hit. This check is repeated each turn until the lantern is found and relit. Once Bogoff has blown it out, he drops it and runs. The Warriors relight it if the Wizard rolls a 5 or 6 in the Power Phase.
One of the Warriors slips in a foul mess on the floor: he has trodden in something a war boar has left behind.

Take a Warrior counter. That Warrior has slipped flat on his face. The whole area is covered in filth, and if Monsters turn up all the Warriors are at -1 to hit for as long as they stay on this board section.
The Warriors stumble across a pile of bones, gold and other jumbled oddments and start rooting around in the dirt.

Roll 1D6. 1-2: the Warriors waste an hour searching. Nothing else happens, but roll 4 dice to represent the passing of time; for each 1, take another Event card. 3-4: they find some gold, resolved using the usual Roleplay Book gold rules. 5-6: each Warrior takes a Treasure card, but the noise attracts trouble. Take an Event card now. If it is not Monsters, shuffle it back and keep drawing until Monsters appear. The maximum number arrives and ambushes the Warriors.
A horde of smelly, squeaking fungoid creatures drops on the Warriors from the roof of the cave.

Each Warrior is attacked by 6 Squiglitz +2 per battle-level. Each Warrior makes a normal damage roll against his attackers, and every Wound caused kills one Squiglit. Each surviving Squiglit then bites that Warrior for 2 Wounds, with no modifiers. Repeat this each turn until none remain. Each dead Squiglit is worth 5 gold.
The ceiling above the Warriors is carved into the snarling face of a massive Orc, and its eyes suddenly glow red.

Each player draws a Warrior counter without looking and places it face down. When all counters are revealed, any player who drew his own Warrior has avoided the attack. Any Warrior not so lucky is engulfed in Orcy energy and suffers 1D3 Wounds per battle-level, with no deductions.
Roll 1D6. On a score of 1, 2 or 3 draw another Event card immediately.
One of the Warriors suddenly feels a sharp pain in the back of his neck and dislodges a tiny red spider.

Take one Warrior counter to see who was bitten and another to see who is the self-proclaimed spider expert. The bitten Warrior then rolls 1D6. 1-3: the spider was poisonous after all. He suffers 2D6 Wounds with no deductions and is at -1 Strength until he drinks a healing potion. 4-5: the toxin acts later. The next time the Wizard rolls a 1 in the Power Phase, the Warrior collapses and may do nothing for that turn. On the following turn he gets up again, but all his characteristics except Wounds are reduced to 1. They then recover by +1 each turn until normal. 6: the toxin speeds up his metabolism, giving him +1 Attacks for the rest of the adventure.
With a deep rumbling sound, the ceiling starts to fall in.

Any Warriors standing next to a wall are hit by rubble and suffer 1D6 Wounds +1 Wound per battle-level. Warriors in the middle of the room are safe. As the dust clears, a secret door is revealed. Place a door on an empty wall and put 1D6 shuffled spare Dungeon cards behind it. When the Warriors reach the last card in that mini deck and resolve any event there, they each gain a Treasure card. The Warriors do not have to explore the secret door if they do not wish to.
Roll 1D6. On a score of 1, 2 or 3 draw another Event card immediately.
Wedged between two bricks in the wall, the Warriors find a faded map.

Roll 1D6. 1: the map is bait for a trap; keep this card, because the next time the Wizard rolls a 1 in the Power Phase the Warriors must take 1D6 Event cards instead of one. 2: the map misleads the Warriors; add three Dungeon cards to the top of the current pile. 3-4: it marks hidden treasure in the next dungeon room entered; on completing the event there, the Warriors gain one extra item of Dungeon Room treasure. 5: it shows a route back to civilisation and reduces the next trip to a Settlement by two weeks. 6: at the next T-junction, the leader may look at the top 1D3 cards of each pile before choosing.
An incautious move kicks up a cloud of luminescent orange spores from the floor.

Each Warrior on this board section rolls 1D6. 1: he falls asleep until the Wizard rolls a 5 or 6 in the Power Phase. 2: he suffers 1D6 Wounds +1 Wound per battle-level, with no modifiers for Toughness or armour. 3: he has a sneezing fit, loses 1 Attack in the next combat, and suffers -1 to hit. 4: no effect. 5: he is healed of 1D6 Wounds +1 Wound per battle-level. 6: he gains +1 Strength for the rest of the adventure; on a further roll of 6, the bonus is permanent.
Take another Event card immediately. If it does not reveal Monsters, resolve that event and keep turning cards until Monsters do turn up.
Each time a Carrion makes a successful hit, roll twice for damage, using 1D6 + 3 each time, and apply both results to the Warrior.

The Grimoire Necris is a source of great necromantic power. If the Dread King is standing next to it and is not pinned, he rerolls spell results of 1 when determining which Necromantic spell he casts. If Gunther Laranscheld starts the Monsters' Phase next to the Grimoire and is not pinned, he may attempt to cast a Necromantic spell on a D6 roll of 4 or more.

Whenever Luthor appears, he makes his attacks immediately after being placed. Then roll 1D6. On a 1 he stays and fights as normal. On 2 or more he vanishes into the darkness and is removed from the board. If he has vanished, every later Unexpected Event also triggers a fresh Luthor attack. If Luthor is in play when Gunther Laranscheld appears, place Luthor next to him and he stays automatically.

Gunther Laranscheld is always protected by Undead guards. When an encounter or event says he appears with Minions, roll 1D6 on the following table: 1 one Tomb Guardian; 2-3 1D6 Skeletons armed with spears; 4 1D6 Zombies; 5 1D6 Ghouls; 6 1D6 Skeletons armed with swords.

When a Zombie Dragon attacks, roll 1D6 to see how many Warriors are affected by its cloud of corrosive gas. If the score is greater than the number of living Warriors, all living Warriors are affected. Each affected Warrior suffers 3D6 Wounds with no deductions for armour. Any Warrior reduced to 0 Wounds by this attack also suffers the Zombie Dragon's Plague and loses 1 Toughness when healed back to life.

When playing an Undead adventure and an Undead Monster has magic armour, use the following Undead Magic Armour table. 1 The Blighted Shield: the Monster gains +1 Toughness, and any spell cast at the bearer is reflected back at the caster on a D6 roll of 6. 2 The Helm of Krell: all Warriors are at -1 to hit the wearer with hand-to-hand and missile attacks; any Warrior who rolls a 1 to hit is seized by madness and may do nothing for a whole turn, and Monsters are at +1 to hit that Warrior. 3 Armour of Warding: the Monster gains Magic Resistance (4). 4 Armour of Blight: the Monster gains Regenerate (2), in addition to any abilities it already possesses. 5 Breastplate of Numas: when the wearer is reduced to zero Wounds, roll 1D6. On 5 or 6 it is not dead and instead has 1D6 Wounds remaining; only on 4 or less does it finally die. 6 The Cursed Armour of Quatar: the Monster loses 1 Attack but gains +3D6 Starting Wounds; no Monster may ever have less than 1 Attack.

When playing an Undead adventure and an Undead Monster has a magic item, use the following Undead Magic Items table. 1 Staff of Flaming Death: gives the Monster the equivalent of Breathe Fire (3). 2 Skull Staff: only a magic-using Monster may have this item, otherwise reroll. The bearer may force Warriors to reroll any Resist Magic or Dispel Magic attempts, and magic items used against the wielder have no effect on a D6 roll of 6, though limited-use items are still expended. 3 The Cursed Books of Har-Ak-Iman: any Warrior adjacent to the bearer suffers -1 to hand-to-hand and missile to hit rolls, and all Warrior spells cast on the same board section cost +1 Power. 4 Bracelet of Khemri: any hand-to-hand attack against the bearer is deflected on a 6 and has no effect, in addition to any other abilities the Monster already has. 5 Amulet of Helsnicht: at the start of each Monsters' Phase roll 1D6. On 1-2 nothing happens, on 3-4 the bearer creates 1D6 Skeletons placed immediately using the normal rules and able to attack that Phase, on 5 the bearer heals 2D6 Wounds up to its Starting Wounds, and on 6 it may immediately cast a single Necromantic spell. 6 Staff of Osiris: any Warrior standing next to the bearer at the start of the Monsters' Phase is hit on 4, 5 or 6 by an attack with Strength equal to that Warrior's battle-level.

When playing an Undead adventure and an Undead Monster has a magic weapon, use the following Undead Magic Weapons table. 1 Dread Blade: any Warrior standing adjacent to the wielder at the end of the turn suffers 3 Fatal Wounds with no deductions. 2 Chaos Tomb Blade: each time a Warrior is reduced to zero Wounds by this blade, roll 1D6; on a 1 he permanently loses 1D3 Wounds. 3 Blade of Resurrection: keep track of the Wounds caused by this weapon; for every 10 Wounds caused, one Skeleton Warrior armed with a sword is created and placed using the normal rules, able to attack in the next Monsters' Phase. 4 Grave Ripper: each hit ignores 1D6 points of armour, and on a roll of 6 one randomly determined piece of armour worn by the target is destroyed. 5 Axes of Nagashizzar: the wielder gains +1 Attack; when a Warrior is reduced to zero Wounds by one of the axes, roll 1D6. On 1 he is dead and cannot be healed by any means, on 2-3 he permanently loses 1D6 Wounds, on 4 he permanently loses 1 Toughness, on 5 he permanently loses 1 Strength, and on 6 he suffers no additional effect. 6 Flail of Skulls: on a natural 6 to hit, the Warrior takes an extra 2D6 Wounds with no deductions.
